attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old man presents to his General Practice concerned about an itchy rash affecting the back of his knees and eyelids. He has had eczema for some time and continues to get flares regularly. He normally uses emollient once per day and betamethasone steroid cream on and off on his legs when it flares. He currently works as an engineer and cannot think of any new triggers in his life. He washes with body lotions and generally just uses one that is on sale at the supermarket. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination he appears well with vitals within normal limits. The rash affecting his popliteal fossa is erythematous, excoriated, scaly and there is evidence of mild lichenification. There is a similar scaly, erythematous eruption across both upper eyelid. There are no signs of infection of either rash.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is correct advice to give to this patient?
options: He should avoid common food allergens
Steroid cream should be applied daily indefinitely
He should use a course of mupirocin 2% topical ointment
The same steroid cream can be used for his legs and face
He should use soap free washes
attributes.explanation:
Based on the rash description, location and his medical history the most likely cause is eczema. Eczema/atopic dermatitis, is a common condition that causes dry, erythematous patches of extremely itchy skin to develop. Management surrounds avoiding triggers and minimising contact with irritants, such as soaps, shampoo, pools with chlorine, itchy clothing (eg. Wool) and dry weather. Soap free washes are recommended to prevent worsening of eczema from chemicals and scents that are added to many body washes. Common ones sold at supermarkets often have chemicals and fragrance added to them, which can cause eczema to flare. He should be advised to avoid these in future and just use plain soap free washes.

Often people with atopic dermatitis have food allergies as well due to their atopic tendencies. However, it is actually quite rare for a food to worsen eczema. If there is a food allergy or intolerance present or worsening eczema it is usually quite obvious and he would notice a flare of his eczema is close relation to the food. Therefore, avoiding all common food allergies would be restrictive and unlikely to help. If he was concerned about food allergy he could see an immunologist or pursue patch testing.

Steroid cream is used topically to reduce the inflammation. Different areas of the body require different strengths of steroid cream, because areas such as the face have thinner and more delicate skin. Therefore, using the same steroid cream for his face and legs is incorrect. Mometasone is a moderate strength steroid used for the body, it should not be applied to the face. He should use weaker steroid compounds such as hydrocortisone for his face. Steroid creams are an important treatment of eczema flares, in this case it is likely that he is not using the steroid cream regularly enough and for long enough to control his eczema as suggested by his "on off use". Steroid cream should be applied regularly and daily during a flare until it is under control, then ideally should be slowly tapered off to prevent another flare upon cessation of the steroid cream. They should never be used daily indefinitely. Topical steroids are safe when used correctly, but if they are used long-term they can cause the skin to become thinner, bruise easier and potentially develop striae.

From the stem there are no signs of infection of his eczema rashes so a course of antibiotics such as mupirocin 2% topical ointment is not indicated, making option C incorrect.
learningPoints: The mainstay of treatment for eczema includes: avoiding triggers, emollient and steroid cream
Steroid creams should not be used indefinitely. They should be used during flares and tapered off appropriately
Soap free washes should be recommended for all people with atopic eczema
references: Dermatitis: Atopic dermatitis. In: Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd.  [online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.tg.org.au
Oakley A. Topical steroid. In: DermNet NZ [online]. 2016. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/topical-steroid/.

Olabi B, Williams HC. Evidence-based management of eczema: five things that should be done more and five things that should be dropped. Curr Opin Allergy Clin Immunol. 2021 Aug 1;21(4):386-393. doi: 10.1097/ACI.0000000000000750. PMID: 33993140. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33993140/
Frazier W, Bhardwaj N. Atopic Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2020 May 15;101(10):590-598. PMID: 32412211. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33993140/
